county_metadata.record_losses: No known courthouse record losses. Young county with intact records.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1911 from Cumberland and Robeson counties. Pre-1911 records split between Cumberland (excellent ROD portal) and Robeson County. One of NC's youngest counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Start with Hoke County ROD online portal. For pre-1911 research, Cumberland County ROD has all records digitized from 1754. FamilySearch has Hoke County records from 1911. NCGenWeb Hoke County page has specialized resources including Longstreet Presbyterian Church Cemetery (232 graves).
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Full System, Imaging System, and Scanned Index Books

source_profiles.source_name: Hoke County Register of Deeds Online
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Online deed search with Full System, Imaging System, and Scanned Index Books. All records from county formation.
Record of Wills with index and images.
Marriage registers from county formation.

source_profiles.source_name: Cumberland County ROD (pre-1911 Hoke area records)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: All pre-1911 records for the Hoke County area are in Cumberland County. Cumberland's ROD portal is one of NC's best — fully digitized from Book 1 (1754).

county_metadata.record_losses: Courthouse fires in 1789 and 1827 destroyed court records from those periods. However, some earlier records survived: marriage records from 1742, land records from 1736, probate records from 1745, court records from 1713 — suggesting duplicate copies or documents preserved elsewhere.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Originally Wickham Precinct of Bath County (1705), renamed Hyde 1712. One of the oldest counties in NC. Pre-1712 records in Bath County papers. As one of the original precincts, Hyde predates most parent county chains.
county_metadata.research_tips: Start with FamilySearch estate records (1735-1944) — exceptional for a burned county. Deeds from 1736 survived despite fires. Marriage records from 1742 are among the oldest in NC. ROD online from 1936/1976 forward; earlier records at office. NC State Archives has burned county resources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: online from 1936/1976 forward; historical records at office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Digitized microfilm. Remarkably early deed records surviving from 1736 despite courthouse fires.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survived 1789 and 1827 fires — may have been stored separately.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Estate records with index and images. Comprehensive coverage despite courthouse fires. May contain records that survived the 1789 fire.
Among the oldest surviving marriage records in NC. Bonds from 1742.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Remarkable survival for a burned county.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Court records from 1713 — among the oldest in NC. Some gaps from courthouse fires but remarkable early survival.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Gaps from 1789 and 1827 fires, but pre-1789 records partially survived.

county_metadata.record_losses: Courthouse fire 1854 destroyed many court records. Vital records, land records, and estate records from before 1854 largely survived through preservation efforts.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Rowan 1788. Pre-1788 records in Rowan County (formed from Anson 1753). Church cemetery records (Centre Presbyterian, Snow Creek Methodist, historic Lutheran churches) have early membership rolls and baptism lists.
county_metadata.research_tips: Start with FamilySearch deeds (survived fire) and wills. Iredell County ROD has excellent online database with deeds from December 1788. Genealogical Society of Iredell County at Iredell County Public Library has family files, Bible records, and indexed materials. Ancestry has 9+ Iredell-specific collections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: online database with all land records from December 1788 to present

source_profiles.source_name: Iredell County Register of Deeds Online
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Online database with deeds, deeds of trust, powers of attorney, plats from December 1788 to present.
Digitized microfilm. Wills and estate records from county formation.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / Genealogical Society of Iredell County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Centre Presbyterian, Snow Creek Methodist, New Perth A.R.P., Hebron Baptist, and historic Lutheran churches with early membership rolls, baptism lists, and cemetery records. Deaths and burials 1898-1994.
Marriage bonds and registers. Marriage index 1893-1934.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1851 from Haywood and Macon counties. The Tuckaseegie River was the dividing line — east was Haywood, west was Macon. Pre-1851 records split between both parent counties. Birth records begin 1913 (NC vital registration).
county_metadata.research_tips: Start with FamilySearch. Jackson County ROD eSearch online from September 1991; earlier records at courthouse. Jackson County Genealogical Society (active since 1991) maintains two-volume Heritage Book. Court records extend to 1843 (pre-formation). For Cherokee-related research, check federal records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: eSearch from September 1991; earlier records at courthouse or on microfilm

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Digitized microfilm. Deeds from shortly after county formation.
County Court minutes (1853-1868), Superior Court Civil actions (1843-1908, 1908-1968), Equity minute docket (1853-1864), guardianship records (1870-1968).
Marriage records from shortly after county formation.
Active since 1991. Maintains Heritage Book (2 volumes). Office Mon-Fri 10am-4pm.

county_metadata.record_losses: Complex history: Early Johnston County records (except deed index) were destroyed when Lenoir County courthouse burned in 1878 and 1880. Records had been transferred when Dobbs County was carved from Johnston in 1758 — Dobbs kept the records, which passed to Lenoir County, where they were destroyed.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Craven 1746. Parent of Dobbs (1758, extinct), which became Wayne and Glasgow/Greene. When Dobbs was created, Johnston's early records went with it and ultimately to Lenoir County, where they burned. Pre-1746 records in Craven County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Johnston County Heritage Center is one of the best-equipped genealogical research facilities in the state — 2,000 books, 800 microfilm reels, 300 maps, 50,000 photos, 400 private collections. Online access to 500,000+ records. Paid research services available ($30/hr, $40 min). The surviving deed index is critical. ROD has online search.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: online record search with images for registered users

source_profiles.source_name: Johnston County Register of Deeds Online
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Online record search with images. The surviving deed index (one of few records that escaped the Lenoir County fires) is critical for early Johnston County research.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Deed index survived; many actual deed records reconstructed.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: One of NC's finest genealogical research facilities. 2,000 books, 800 microfilm reels, 300 maps/atlases, 50,000 photographs, 400 private collections. Online access to 500,000+ Johnston County records. Paid research: $30/hr, $40 minimum.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Critical resource for burned county research.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Will abstracts with index and images. Important for burned county — includes reconstructed records.
Marriage registers from 1851. Pre-1851 marriage bonds largely lost.

county_metadata.record_losses: Courthouse burned 1862 during a Civil War battle. Many court records destroyed. However, deed records 1779-1901 with indexes through 1959 have been preserved.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Craven 1779. Pre-1779 records in Craven County. The 1862 courthouse destruction during Civil War makes Craven County records and published abstracts essential for early Jones County research.
county_metadata.research_tips: Zae Hargett Gwynn's 'Abstracts of Records of Jones County, 1779-1868' is ESSENTIAL — covers deeds, wills, tax lists, guardian accounts, census records, land grants, and marriage records for the critical pre-fire period. Available on Ancestry and from Southern Historical Press. FamilySearch has surviving deeds from 1779. NC Genealogical Society has estate file indexes.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: eSearch with indexes from 1978 and images from 2001; earlier records at office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Digitized microfilm. Deeds with comprehensive index 1779-1959. Survived the 1862 courthouse fire.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Deed records survived — one of the few record types that made it through the fire.

source_profiles.source_name: Abstracts of Records of Jones County, 1779-1868 (Gwynn)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: ESSENTIAL burned county resource. Published abstracts by Zae Hargett Gwynn covering deeds, wills, tax lists, guardian accounts, census records, land grants, and marriage records. Covers the critical pre-fire period.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published abstracts provide access to records otherwise destroyed in 1862.

source_profiles.source_name: North Carolina Estate Files — Jones County Index
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Free online index. Helps navigate probate records for this burned county.
Marriage registers from 1851 forward. Pre-1862 bonds partially lost in fire.
